questions.text: A young male presents with unilateral epistaxis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Unilateral epistaxis in a young male (adolescent) is a classic presentation of juvenile nasal angiofibroma (JNA), a vascular tumor requiring rule-out by nasal endoscopy. Septal deviation and foreign body typically cause obstruction rather than recurrent bleeding, and allergic rhinitis/nasal polyps are usually bilateral.

questions.text: A patient has bone conduction better than air conduction on the right ear (negative Rinne). On Weber's test, sound is heard better in the right ear. What is the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A negative Rinne test (bone conduction > air conduction) on the right ear indicates a conductive problem in that ear, and Weber lateralizing to the same (right) ear confirms this, since sound lateralizes toward the affected ear in conductive hearing loss. In sensorineural loss, Rinne would remain positive (air > bone) and Weber would lateralize to the opposite, better-hearing ear.

questions.text: What tuning fork frequency is used for hearing tests?
questions.explanation: The standard tuning fork used for Weber's and Rinne's tests is 512 Hz, providing a balance between adequate duration of vibration and clinical accuracy. Lower frequencies (128–256 Hz) are felt more than heard, while higher frequencies decay too quickly for reliable bedside testing.

questions.text: A child presents with a midline neck lump that moves when they stick out their tongue. What is the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A thyroglossal cyst is a midline neck lump that characteristically moves upward on tongue protrusion due to its embryological connection to the foramen cecum. Thyroid isthmus swelling moves with swallowing but not tongue protrusion, dermoid cysts are non-tender and mobile without this movement, and branchial cysts are lateral, not midline.

questions.text: A 55-year-old male smoker presents with hoarseness of voice persisting for 3 weeks. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
originalOptions: Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
questions.correctAnswer: D) Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
questions.correctAnswerText: Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
questions.explanation: Any smoker with hoarseness lasting more than 3 weeks is a red flag requiring urgent ENT referral for fiberoptic endoscopy to look for laryngeal tumors, as emphasized directly in the lecture. Antibiotics, PPIs, or reassurance risk delaying diagnosis of a potentially malignant cause.

questions.text: A young male teacher presents with hoarseness of voice for 3 weeks with a history of prolonged vocal strain. Laryngoscopy reveals a smooth, unilateral lesion on the vocal cord. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A vocal cord polyp typically presents as a unilateral, smooth lesion following a single episode of vocal strain or trauma, unlike nodules which are bilateral and related to chronic voice abuse (e.g., singers/teachers using their voice repetitively).

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT an early sign of death?
questions.explanation: Livor mortis, rigor mortis, and pallor mortis are recognized postmortem changes that develop progressively over time and are used forensically to estimate time and circumstances of death. Loss of peripheral pulse occurs immediately at the moment of cardiac arrest rather than developing afterward, so it is a clinical sign of death, not a postmortem change. This distinction is why the lecture emphasized rigor mortis, hypostasis, and decomposition as the key diagnostic postmortem changes.

questions.text: Traumatic asphyxia results from severe injury to which part of the body?
questions.explanation: Traumatic asphyxia is defined as compression on the chest and abdomen by external force, impairing the mechanical ability to breathe. This differs from strangulation/hanging (neck level) and smothering (mouth and nose level), which are other forms of violent asphyxia acting at different anatomical levels.

questions.text: In forensic medicine, strangulation can be categorized into which manner of death?
questions.explanation: Strangulation (compression of neck structures by ligature or hands) is mostly homicidal, unlike hanging, which is mostly suicidal but can be accidental or homicidal. This distinction in manner of death is forensically important because it directly affects legal classification and investigation of the case.

questions.text: Which type of injury is not caused by a blunt object but rather by a sharp object, typically resulting in clean, well-defined wound edges?
questions.explanation: Incisions/cut wounds are caused by a clean, sharp-edged object such as a knife or glass splinter, producing regular edges, clear margins, and no tissue bridging. In contrast, lacerations, abrasions, and contusions all result from blunt trauma and typically show irregular edges, contused margins, and tissue bridging.

questions.text: What is the approximate time window during which myocardial cells can be harvested after cardiac arrest?
questions.explanation: Different tissues and organs remain viable for different durations after death, since cells do not die instantly and organs vary in their tolerance to anoxia (the lecture notes the brain survives only 5-7 minutes and the kidney about 45 minutes). This variability explains why timing is critical for organ/tissue donation and harvesting after cardiac arrest.

questions.text: Stab wounds can be classified into which of the following categories?
questions.explanation: Stab wounds can result from a purely sharp instrument, a blunt instrument, or a blunt object with a pointed tip (as illustrated by the example of a worker falling onto a round-tipped iron rod), since what matters legally and clinically is the depth and mechanism of penetration rather than the object's overall sharpness. This is why all three categories are considered valid classifications of stab wounds in forensic assessment.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a cause of an incisional (cut) wound?
questions.explanation: Incisions/cut wounds are caused by a clean, sharp-edged object such as a knife, razor, or glass splinter/broken glass. A rock is a blunt object, so injury from it produces a laceration (irregular tear-like wound), not an incision — making it the exception among the choices.

questions.text: The mechanism of rigor mortis involves decreased ATP leading to which direct biochemical event in muscle cells?
options: Binding of actin and myosin fibres into a gel
Calcium efflux from the sarcoplasmic reticulum
originalOptions: Binding of actin and myosin fibres into a gel
Calcium efflux from the sarcoplasmic reticulum
questions.correctAnswer: B) Binding of actin and myosin fibres into a gel
questions.correctAnswerText: Binding of actin and myosin fibres into a gel
questions.explanation: After death, anaerobic respiration causes lactic acid buildup and increasing acidity; combined with low ATP, this causes the actin and myosin fibres to bind together and form a stiff gel, producing rigor mortis. This is why rigor mortis preserves and reflects the position the person died in, rather than reflecting later body movement.

questions.text: Smothering is best defined as which of the following?
options: Compression on neck structures by ligature
Compression on neck structures by the weight of the body
Closure of the nostrils and mouth at the same time from outside
Compression on the chest and abdomen by external force
Occlusion of the respiratory passages from inside
originalOptions: Compression on neck structures by ligature
Compression on neck structures by the weight of the body
Closure of the nostrils and mouth at the same time from outside
Compression on the chest and abdomen by external force
Occlusion of the respiratory passages from inside
questions.correctAnswer: C) Closure of the nostrils and mouth at the same time from outside
questions.correctAnswerText: Closure of the nostrils and mouth at the same time from outside
questions.explanation: Smothering occurs at the level of the mouth and nose, involving external closure of both openings simultaneously, and is mostly suicidal though it can be homicidal. This differs from gagging, which produces a similar effect but from inside the mouth (e.g., a cloth pushed into the mouth and pharynx).

questions.text: A wound characterized by irregular, tear-like edges caused by blunt trauma is best classified as which type of injury?
questions.explanation: Lacerations are irregular tear-like wounds caused by blunt trauma, requiring a firm structure (such as bone) beneath the skin to allow the tissue to tear and the skin to open. This contrasts with incisions, which have regular edges and clear margins from a sharp object, and abrasions, which are superficial scrapes of the epidermis only.

questions.text: The color of skin discoloration seen in postmortem hypostasis is primarily determined by which factor?
options: The ambient temperature at the death scene
The position in which the body was found
The chemical state of hemoglobin molecules
The total blood volume remaining in the vessels
The time elapsed since cardiac arrest
originalOptions: The ambient temperature at the death scene
The position in which the body was found
The chemical state of hemoglobin molecules
The total blood volume remaining in the vessels
The time elapsed since cardiac arrest
questions.correctAnswer: C) The chemical state of hemoglobin molecules
questions.correctAnswerText: The chemical state of hemoglobin molecules
questions.explanation: Postmortem hypostasis color depends on the chemical state of hemoglobin (e.g., carboxyhemoglobin, methemoglobin), which is why, for example, carbon monoxide poisoning classically produces a pink or light red discoloration rather than the usual bluish-purple color. This makes hypostasis color a useful clue for suspecting certain causes of death, such as CO poisoning.

questions.text: If a patient has a visual acuity of 6/12, what does this mean?
options: They can see at 6 meters what a person with normal vision can see at 12 meters
They can see at 12 meters what a person with normal vision can see at 6 meters
Their vision is better than normal, resolving details invisible to the average eye at 6 meters
They have normal visual acuity equivalent to 6/6
They can only perceive hand movements at a distance of 6 meters
originalOptions: They can see at 6 meters what a person with normal vision can see at 12 meters
They can see at 12 meters what a person with normal vision can see at 6 meters
Their vision is better than normal, resolving details invisible to the average eye at 6 meters
They have normal visual acuity equivalent to 6/6
They can only perceive hand movements at a distance of 6 meters
questions.correctAnswer: A) They can see at 6 meters what a person with normal vision can see at 12 meters
questions.correctAnswerText: They can see at 6 meters what a person with normal vision can see at 12 meters
questions.explanation: 6/12 = test distance/normal distance, meaning reduced acuity, not enhanced. B reverses the fraction (would mean better-than-normal vision), C and D wrongly describe normal or supranormal vision, and E describes near-blindness, far worse than 6/12.

questions.text: This test is used to asses (The image shows: A circle of green dots containing the number 12 made of orange dots) ?
questions.explanation: This is an Ishihara pseudoisochromatic plate, used specifically for color vision testing. A uses the Snellen chart, B uses a light source, C uses confrontation/perimetry, and E uses contrast charts — none use colored dot plates.

questions.text: What condition causes acute painful loss of vision?
questions.explanation: Only angle-closure glaucoma is both acute and painful. A and E are acute but painless, while C and D are painless and gradual, not acute.

questions.text: All are causes of this sign except (The image shows: A red eye) ?
questions.explanation: Macular edema is a retinal/central vision problem, not a cause of ocular redness. A–D all directly cause a red eye through conjunctival, corneal, or intraocular inflammation/hemorrhage.

questions.text: What is the term for pupils of different sizes?
questions.explanation: Anisocoria specifically means unequal pupil size. Nystagmus is involuntary eye movement, ptosis is lid drooping, diplopia is double vision, and RAPD is an abnormal light reflex — none describe pupil size difference.

questions.text: What is the visual field exam called?
questions.explanation: The confrontation test is the bedside visual field exam. A tests color, B tests for RAPD, D tests acuity, and E examines the fundus — all test different functions.

questions.text: What are the three components involved in accommodation?
questions.correctAnswer: A) Accommodation, pupillary constriction, and convergence
questions.explanation: The near reflex triad is lens accommodation + pupillary constriction + convergence. B and C swap in divergence (the opposite of convergence), D swaps in dilation (opposite of constriction), and E replaces accommodation with visual acuity, which isn't part of the triad.

questions.text: The limbus houses germinative stem cells that maintain the corneal epithelium. What is the primary function of this layer?
options: Anchoring the epithelium via desmosomes only
Providing mechanical strength via collagen fibrils
Regeneration and proliferation of epithelial cells
originalOptions: Anchoring the epithelium via desmosomes only
Providing mechanical strength via collagen fibrils
Regeneration and proliferation of epithelial cells
questions.correctAnswer: E) Regeneration and proliferation of epithelial cells
questions.correctAnswerText: Regeneration and proliferation of epithelial cells
questions.explanation: Limbal stem cells continuously regenerate the corneal epithelium, allowing rapid repair after abrasion by cell spreading and proliferation. A is incomplete, as anchoring also involves hemidesmosomes and fibrils, not desmosomes alone. B is wrong — the endothelium instead repairs by cell spreading alone with loss of density, no regeneration. C and D describe Bowman's layer and the trabecular meshwork, unrelated structures.

questions.text: Which of the following statements best describes Alogia?
options: It refers to speech that lacks content but is delivered fluently and without hesitation
It involves both poverty in speech output and poverty in the content of speech
It refers to speech that is rich in content but delivered in an impoverished, halting manner
It refers to an excessive amount of speech with overly detailed content
It refers to speech that is normal in amount but disorganized in structure
originalOptions: It refers to speech that lacks content but is delivered fluently and without hesitation
It involves both poverty in speech output and poverty in the content of speech
It refers to speech that is rich in content but delivered in an impoverished, halting manner
It refers to an excessive amount of speech with overly detailed content
It refers to speech that is normal in amount but disorganized in structure
questions.correctAnswer: B) It involves both poverty in speech output and poverty in the content of speech
questions.correctAnswerText: It involves both poverty in speech output and poverty in the content of speech
questions.explanation: Alogia (poverty of speech) is a negative symptom involving reduction in both the amount of speech produced and the richness of its content. This distinguishes it from disorders of speech delivery alone, where content may still be preserved.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a component of thought content?
questions.explanation: Thought content covers delusions, obsessions, overvalued ideas, and suicidal/homicidal ideation. Hallucinations are a disturbance of perception, not thought content, so they are assessed separately under the perception domain of the MSE.

questions.text: What is the difference between mood and affect?
options: Mood is a temporary emotional state, while affect is a persistent, long-lasting state
Mood is the external expression of emotions, while affect is the internal and sustained emotional state
Mood and affect are interchangeable terms describing the same clinical finding
Mood is the internal and sustained emotional state, while affect is the external expression of emotions
Mood refers only to observable facial expression, while affect refers to the patient's subjective report
originalOptions: Mood is a temporary emotional state, while affect is a persistent, long-lasting state
Mood is the external expression of emotions, while affect is the internal and sustained emotional state
Mood and affect are interchangeable terms describing the same clinical finding
Mood is the internal and sustained emotional state, while affect is the external expression of emotions
Mood refers only to observable facial expression, while affect refers to the patient's subjective report
questions.correctAnswer: D) Mood is the internal and sustained emotional state, while affect is the external expression of emotions
questions.correctAnswerText: Mood is the internal and sustained emotional state, while affect is the external expression of emotions
questions.explanation: Mood is the patient's subjective, sustained internal emotional climate, typically elicited by asking how they feel. Affect is the examiner's objective observation of the moment-to-moment external expression of that emotion during the interview.

questions.text: A 68-year-old man with a resectable colon cancer refuses a life-saving surgery after being fully informed of the risks, benefits, and consequences of declining treatment. He understands his diagnosis and can clearly explain his reasoning. Which ethical principle is most directly being exercised by the patient?
questions.explanation: Autonomy is the patient's right to make informed decisions about their own care, including the right to refuse even life-saving treatment, provided they have decision-making capacity. The physician's role is to ensure understanding and document the discussion, not to override the choice.

questions.text: A psychiatrist prescribes a sedating antipsychotic at an unnecessarily high dose, causing the patient significant drowsiness and a fall, without any added therapeutic benefit compared to a lower dose. Which ethical principle has been breached?
questions.explanation: Non-maleficence means "do no harm." Causing avoidable harm, such as unnecessary side effects or injury from an excessive dose that provided no additional benefit, is a direct breach of this principle rather than of beneficence, which concerns actively promoting the patient's welfare.

questions.text: A psychiatrist notices that a patient with depression has been missing follow-up visits and personally arranges transportation and a follow-up call to ensure the patient continues treatment and recovers. This action best reflects which principle?
questions.explanation: Beneficence is the duty to actively act in the patient's best interest, such as providing effective treatment, preventing harm, and supporting recovery. This differs from non-maleficence, which is about avoiding harm rather than actively promoting benefit.

questions.text: A hospital allocates its limited number of psychiatric inpatient beds based on clinical urgency rather than on patients' social status or ability to pay. This practice best reflects which ethical principle?
questions.explanation: Justice refers to fair and equal treatment, including equal access to care regardless of social standing, and avoiding discrimination or use of influence to obtain preferential treatment. Allocating resources by clinical need rather than status exemplifies this principle.

questions.text: A patient in psychotherapy discloses that he purchased a weapon and plans to harm a specific coworker once discharged. According to the limits of confidentiality discussed in psychiatric ethics, what is the most appropriate action?
options: Maintain strict confidentiality regardless of the threat, since disclosure would breach trust
Disclose only the minimum necessary information to protect the identified person at risk
Disclose the patient's entire treatment record to the police
Wait until the patient is discharged before taking any action
Ask the patient's family to decide whether to report it
originalOptions: Maintain strict confidentiality regardless of the threat, since disclosure would breach trust
Disclose only the minimum necessary information to protect the identified person at risk
Disclose the patient's entire treatment record to the police
Wait until the patient is discharged before taking any action
Ask the patient's family to decide whether to report it
questions.correctAnswer: B) Disclose only the minimum necessary information to protect the identified person at risk
questions.correctAnswerText: Disclose only the minimum necessary information to protect the identified person at risk
questions.explanation: Confidentiality may be overridden when there is a serious risk of harm to others, such as a specific, credible threat of violence. The guiding principle is to disclose only the minimum information necessary to protect the person at risk, not the full record.

questions.text: All of the following are required for a patient to be considered to have decision-making capacity EXCEPT:
options: Understanding relevant information about the treatment
Appreciating how the information applies to their own situation
Reasoning about the available treatment options
originalOptions: Understanding relevant information about the treatment
Appreciating how the information applies to their own situation
Reasoning about the available treatment options
questions.explanation: Capacity requires understanding, appreciation, reasoning, and the ability to communicate a consistent choice. A psychiatric diagnosis alone does not equal lack of capacity, since capacity is decision-specific and can fluctuate independently of diagnosis.

questions.text: A court legally determines that a patient is unable to manage his own financial affairs and appoints a guardian. This determination is best described as an assessment of the patient's:
questions.explanation: Competence is a legal determination made by a court and tends to be more globally stable, whereas capacity is a clinical assessment made by physicians that is decision-specific and can change over time.

questions.text: Which ethical principle serves as the basis for informed consent, truth-telling, and confidentiality in the therapeutic relationship?
questions.explanation: Autonomy underlies a patient's right to receive truthful information, give informed consent, and have their disclosures kept confidential, since all of these depend on respecting the patient's self-determination and control over their own care.

questions.text: A psychiatrist prescribes a new antidepressant to a patient during a rushed clinic visit. The consultation only covers dosage and administration timing, and the patient is discharged without a clear explanation of the possible adverse effects, discovering them only after experiencing severe symptoms at home. Which of the following best represents a breach of the principle of non-maleficence in this scenario?
options: The patient did not fully understand the side effects of the medication
The patient was not given the option to choose an alternative treatment
The psychiatrist did not verify the patient's insurance coverage for the drug
The patient's family was not informed of the diagnosis
The consultation lasted less time than the patient expected
originalOptions: The patient did not fully understand the side effects of the medication
The patient was not given the option to choose an alternative treatment
The psychiatrist did not verify the patient's insurance coverage for the drug
The patient's family was not informed of the diagnosis
The consultation lasted less time than the patient expected
questions.correctAnswer: A) The patient did not fully understand the side effects of the medication
questions.correctAnswerText: The patient did not fully understand the side effects of the medication
questions.explanation: A is correct because non-maleficence means actively preventing avoidable harm, and failing to explain side effects left the patient unable to recognize or avoid the harm that followed. B relates to autonomy (right to choose among options), not harm prevention. C is an administrative/logistical issue with no ethical bearing on harm. D concerns confidentiality/family involvement, not non-maleficence. E is simply a matter of consultation length, not an ethical breach on its own.

questions.text: A 66-year-old man is brought in for evaluation of memory difficulty. During the interview, he gives the exact same response to every question asked, regardless of its content. This finding is most suggestive of which underlying process?
questions.explanation: Giving the same fixed response regardless of the question (perseveration) is a cognitive sign often associated with organic brain pathology such as dementia or frontal lobe damage, rather than a primary thought disorder seen in psychotic or mood disorders.

questions.text: A patient in an acute manic episode rapidly shifts from topic to topic, with each idea loosely connected to the previous one, and later begins stringing words together based on their sound rather than their meaning. Which of the following is NOT a thought process typically seen in this presentation?
questions.explanation: Flight of ideas, clang associations, tangentiality, and pressured speech are all disturbances of thought process (form), commonly seen in mania. Suicidal thoughts belong to thought content, not thought process, making it the outlier among the options.

questions.text: Which of the following is a component of thought content?
questions.explanation: Thought content refers to what the patient is actually thinking about, including delusions, obsessions, and suicidal or homicidal ideation. The other options belong to different MSE domains: cognition (attention, orientation) or appearance/behavior/speech, not thought content.

questions.text: Which imaging modality utilizes ionizing radiation to generate diagnostic images?
questions.explanation: CT uses x-rays (ionizing radiation) to construct cross-sectional "slices" of the body, exactly like a conventional radiograph. MRI and ultrasound, in all their sequences/modes, do not use ionizing radiation, making them safer alternatives for repeated imaging.

questions.text: An erect chest radiograph shows a thin white visceral pleural line with no lung markings peripheral to it, and an adjacent area that is darker than the surrounding lung. Which pathology produces this radiolucent appearance?
options: Rheumatoid arthritis with soft tissue swelling
originalOptions: Rheumatoid arthritis with soft tissue swelling
questions.explanation: Pneumothorax is gas within the pleural space; because it contains no vessels or lymphatics, it appears markedly radiolucent (dark) compared to aerated lung, which still contains vascular and bronchial structures. Pleural effusion and heart failure instead produce opacity (whiteness) on chest x-ray, the opposite finding.

questions.text: On a CT scan, which structures appear white?
questions.explanation: CT terminology describes density relative to surrounding tissue as hyperdense (white), hypodense (black), or isodense (equal), with bone being a classic hyperdense example. "Radiolucent" is the equivalent x-ray term and "hypoechoic" is an ultrasound term, so applying them to CT is a common terminology mix-up.

questions.text: Which of the following best defines hydrocephalus?
options: Obstruction of CSF absorption at the arachnoid granulations following subarachnoid hemorrhage
Overproduction of CSF by a choroid plexus papilloma
Obstruction of CSF flow at the foramen of Monro by a colloid cyst
Compensatory dilation of the ventricles secondary to brain atrophy
Increase in the volume of CSF causing distension of the cerebral ventricles
originalOptions: Obstruction of CSF absorption at the arachnoid granulations following subarachnoid hemorrhage
Overproduction of CSF by a choroid plexus papilloma
Obstruction of CSF flow at the foramen of Monro by a colloid cyst
Compensatory dilation of the ventricles secondary to brain atrophy
Increase in the volume of CSF causing distension of the cerebral ventricles
questions.correctAnswer: E) Increase in the volume of CSF causing distension of the cerebral ventricles
questions.correctAnswerText: Increase in the volume of CSF causing distension of the cerebral ventricles
questions.explanation: Hydrocephalus is defined as ventriculomegaly resulting from increased CSF volume, regardless of the underlying cause. Options A, B, and C describe specific mechanisms/causes (communicating vs. non-communicating types) rather than the definition itself, while option D describes ex-vacuo ventricular enlargement from atrophy, a mimicker that is not true hydrocephalus since CSF volume is not increased.

questions.text: A patient with symmetric small joint involvement shows the following radiographic finding. Which abnormality is characteristic of rheumatoid arthritis rather than osteoarthritis?
questions.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis characteristically produces marginal erosions, soft tissue swelling, juxta-articular then generalized osteoporosis, and symmetric joint space narrowing. Osteophytes, subchondral cysts, and asymmetric narrowing instead point to osteoarthritis, while sacroiliitis is the earliest radiological finding in ankylosing spondylitis.

questions.text: On MRI, structures that appear white are described using which term?
questions.explanation: MRI signal intensity is described as hyperintense (white), hypointense (black), or isointense (equal signal), unlike CT which uses "hyperdense/hypodense" or ultrasound which uses "hyperechoic/hypoechoic." Mixing up these modality-specific terms is a classic exam trap.

questions.text: Which of the following imaging modalities relies on ionizing radiation, unlike the others listed?
questions.explanation: The conventional radiograph utilizes ionizing radiation to produce images, the same principle CT is built on. Ultrasound and MRI, regardless of sequence or mode, do not use ionizing radiation, which is why they are preferred when repeated imaging or radiation avoidance is needed.

questions.text: Which imaging modality avoids ionizing radiation by instead using a large, powerful magnet to generate images?
questions.explanation: MRI does not use ionizing radiation; instead, the scanner consists of a large, powerful magnet in which the patient lies, and images are generated using sequences such as T1, T2, FLAIR, and DWI. CT, radiographs, fluoroscopy, and mammography all rely on x-rays.

questions.text: A patient presents with a new cough. What is the most appropriate initial imaging test to evaluate the chest?
questions.explanation: For any chest complaint, the chest x-ray is the first study obtained, since it is fast, widely available, and gives a broad initial assessment. CT is reserved for further characterization, ultrasound is mainly used for pleural effusion follow-up or procedures, and MRI of the chest is rarely performed.

questions.text: Which ossicle's footplate is attached directly to the oval window, transmitting sound into the inner ear?
questions.explanation: The stapes footplate sits on the oval window and pushes on it to generate perilymph waves in the cochlea, continuing the sound conduction pathway. The malleus and incus are earlier links in the ossicular chain but do not directly contact the oval window.

questions.text: A 70-year-old poorly controlled diabetic presents with severe otalgia and granulation tissue in the ear canal. Which diagnosis must be suspected?
questions.explanation: Severe otalgia in an elderly patient with uncontrolled diabetes is a classic red flag for necrotizing (malignant) otitis externa, an aggressive infection that can extend to the skull base. Acute otitis externa and media are far more common but do not carry this specific risk profile of severe pain plus uncontrolled diabetes.

questions.text: Shedding of otoconia (crystals) from the utricle into the semicircular canals is the underlying mechanism of which condition?
questions.explanation: BPPV results from displaced otoconia (crystals) from the utricle entering the semicircular canals, causing brief seconds-long vertigo with position change. Ménière's disease involves endolymphatic hydrops instead, and vestibular neuritis is due to inflammation, not crystal displacement.

questions.text: A patient reports recurrent vertigo lasting hours, associated with fluctuating unilateral hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The triad of episodic vertigo (hours), fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness is characteristic of Ménière's disease (endolymphatic hydrops). BPPV lasts seconds and vestibular neuritis lasts days, and neither is typically associated with hearing loss.

questions.text: Vesicles in the ear canal accompanied by ipsilateral facial nerve palsy should raise suspicion for which condition?
questions.explanation: Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster oticus) presents with vesicles in the ear canal, facial nerve palsy, and sometimes vertigo or sensorineural hearing loss. This combination of vesicles plus facial palsy is not typical of the other otological conditions listed.

questions.text: On Weber's test, sound lateralizes to the right ear. Rinne's test on the right shows bone conduction greater than air conduction. What is the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Lateralization to the affected ear on Weber's test combined with a negative Rinne's test (bone conduction greater than air conduction) on that same side confirms a conductive hearing loss in the right ear. Sensorineural loss would instead lateralize away from the affected ear with a positive Rinne's test.

questions.text: What is the primary function of the Eustachian tube?
options: Transmission of vibration to the cochlea
Ventilation and equalization of middle ear pressure
originalOptions: Transmission of vibration to the cochlea
Ventilation and equalization of middle ear pressure
questions.correctAnswer: D) Ventilation and equalization of middle ear pressure
questions.correctAnswerText: Ventilation and equalization of middle ear pressure
questions.explanation: The Eustachian tube connects the middle ear to the nasopharynx and functions to ventilate the middle ear space, equalizing its pressure with atmospheric pressure. Sound amplification and vibration transmission are functions of the ossicles, not the Eustachian tube.

questions.text: During an emergency airway obstruction, a cricothyrotomy is performed through which structure?
questions.explanation: The cricothyroid membrane, located between the thyroid and cricoid cartilages, is the site used for emergency cricothyrotomy due to its superficial and relatively avascular location. The cricoid cartilage and thyroid isthmus are nearby structures but are not the incision site for this procedure.

questions.text: A 55-year-old smoker presents with hoarseness of voice persisting for more than 3 weeks. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start proton pump inhibitor and observe
Voice rest and follow-up in 1 month
Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
originalOptions: Start proton pump inhibitor and observe
Voice rest and follow-up in 1 month
Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
questions.correctAnswer: E) Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
questions.correctAnswerText: Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
questions.explanation: Persistent hoarseness beyond 3 weeks in a smoker is a red flag for laryngeal malignancy and warrants urgent ENT referral for fiberoptic endoscopy to visualize the vocal cords and rule out tumors. Reassurance, antibiotics, or symptomatic measures risk delaying diagnosis of a potentially neoplastic process.

questions.text: Tenderness elicited on palpation of the tragus is most suggestive of which condition?
questions.explanation: Tragal tenderness on palpation is a classic clinical sign of otitis externa, as inflammation of the external auditory canal makes movement of the pinna/tragus painful. Acute otitis media, a middle ear condition, does not typically produce tragal tenderness since the external ear is not primarily inflamed.

questions.text: Death is defined as the complete, irreversible cessation of the integrated function of which three systems?
questions.explanation: Death is defined as complete, irreversible cessation of the integrated function of the central nervous system, respiratory system, and cardiovascular system, reflected by absence of brain stem reflexes. This triad forms the basis for clinically confirming death before postmortem changes even begin.

questions.text: What is the main clinical rationale for recognizing early postmortem changes such as rigor mortis, hypostasis, and decomposition?
options: To determine the exact cause of death immediately
To confirm death and avoid unnecessary resuscitation attempts
To estimate the victim's exact age at death
To identify the weapon used in an assault
To calculate the total blood loss at the scene
originalOptions: To determine the exact cause of death immediately
To confirm death and avoid unnecessary resuscitation attempts
To estimate the victim's exact age at death
To identify the weapon used in an assault
To calculate the total blood loss at the scene
questions.correctAnswer: B) To confirm death and avoid unnecessary resuscitation attempts
questions.correctAnswerText: To confirm death and avoid unnecessary resuscitation attempts
questions.explanation: Recognizing these signs confirms that a person is already dead, so there is no need to perform CPR, defibrillation, or give adrenaline, saving time and effort. It also has a social value, since attempting resuscitation on an obviously dead body in front of family can be inappropriate.

questions.text: The pale areas seen at pressure points within a region of postmortem hypostasis are best explained by:
options: Increased hemoglobin breakdown at those sites
Localized rigor mortis affecting the skin
Blood being unable to accumulate due to compression at those points
Early putrefaction changes at contact points
Reduced carboxyhemoglobin formation at those areas
originalOptions: Increased hemoglobin breakdown at those sites
Localized rigor mortis affecting the skin
Blood being unable to accumulate due to compression at those points
Early putrefaction changes at contact points
Reduced carboxyhemoglobin formation at those areas
questions.correctAnswer: C) Blood being unable to accumulate due to compression at those points
questions.correctAnswerText: Blood being unable to accumulate due to compression at those points
questions.explanation: This phenomenon, called pressure pallor, occurs because pressure at contact points squeezes blood out and prevents it from stagnating there, unlike the surrounding dependent skin. It is a useful clue for reconstructing the body's position or contact surfaces after death.

questions.text: Rigor mortis characteristically begins first in which muscle group before descending through the body?
options: Muscles of the hands and fingers
Small muscles of the face and jaw
originalOptions: Muscles of the hands and fingers
Small muscles of the face and jaw
questions.correctAnswer: D) Small muscles of the face and jaw
questions.correctAnswerText: Small muscles of the face and jaw
questions.explanation: Rigor mortis typically starts in the small muscles of the face and jaw before progressing downward through the body. Because it preserves the position at the time of death, its distribution can help reconstruct how the body was positioned when the person died.

questions.text: A body found suspended by a ligature around the neck, with compression caused by the weight of the body, is most consistent with which manner of death?
questions.explanation: Hanging is compression of the neck by the weight of the body (or part of it) and is mostly suicidal, though it can occasionally be accidental. This contrasts with strangulation by ligature or hands, which is mostly homicidal.

questions.text: Choking, as a mechanical anoxic death, results from occlusion of the respiratory passages at which level?
options: Nose and mouth level, from outside
Chest and abdomen level, by external force
originalOptions: Nose and mouth level, from outside
Chest and abdomen level, by external force
questions.explanation: Choking is the mechanical anoxic death caused by occlusion of the respiratory passages from inside, at the level of the larynx, such as by a swallowed foreign object. This distinguishes it from smothering (mouth/nose, external) and traumatic asphyxia (chest/abdomen).

questions.text: The presence of froth at the mouth when a body is retrieved from water is most suggestive of:
options: Death from traumatic asphyxia before submersion
originalOptions: Death from traumatic asphyxia before submersion
questions.explanation: If froth is found coming from the mouth when the body is pulled from the water, this indicates the person was still alive and breathing when submerged, i.e., antemortem drowning. This distinction is forensically essential to determine whether the person fell into water before or after death.

questions.text: In a burn victim, a blood carboxyhemoglobin (CO) level above approximately 10% suggests which of the following?
options: The person died before the fire started
The person had chronic COPD prior to death
The person was alive during the fire (antemortem burning)
The fire was electrical rather than chemical in origin
The body was moved into the fire after death
originalOptions: The person died before the fire started
The person had chronic COPD prior to death
The person was alive during the fire (antemortem burning)
The fire was electrical rather than chemical in origin
The body was moved into the fire after death
questions.correctAnswer: C) The person was alive during the fire (antemortem burning)
questions.correctAnswerText: The person was alive during the fire (antemortem burning)
questions.explanation: A COHb level above roughly 10% indicates the person was breathing combustion smoke and therefore alive during the fire, confirming antemortem burning. This is analogous to using froth to confirm antemortem drowning, both distinguishing whether the person was alive at the time of the fatal event.

questions.text: "Gagging" differs from "smothering" mainly in that gagging involves closure of the airway:
options: At the neck level rather than the mouth
By a ligature rather than by hands
At the chest level rather than the mouth
From inside the mouth rather than from outside
Due to natural disease rather than external force
originalOptions: At the neck level rather than the mouth
By a ligature rather than by hands
At the chest level rather than the mouth
From inside the mouth rather than from outside
Due to natural disease rather than external force
questions.correctAnswer: D) From inside the mouth rather than from outside
questions.correctAnswerText: From inside the mouth rather than from outside
questions.explanation: Gagging resembles smothering but occurs from inside, such as when a piece of cloth (the gag) is pushed into the mouth and pressed backward, closing the mouth and nose internally rather than externally. Both act at the same anatomical level (mouth and nose) but differ in the direction/source of occlusion.

questions.text: Which wound feature at the wound bottom is most useful in distinguishing a laceration from an incised (cut) wound?
questions.explanation: Lacerations, caused by blunt trauma, typically show tissue bridging at the wound bottom because some tougher tissue strands resist tearing, along with irregular edges and contused margins. Incised wounds, from sharp objects, characteristically lack bridging and instead show regular edges with a clean, clear margin.

questions.text: A patient underwent uncomplicated cataract surgery three weeks ago and now presents with a red, painful eye. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A patient with recent cataract surgery presenting with red, painful eye must be assumed to have endophthalmitis until proven otherwise, as it is a sight-threatening postoperative infection requiring urgent management. Allergic conjunctivitis and blepharitis do not carry this same post-surgical urgency.

questions.text: During the swinging light test, light is moved to the affected eye and both pupils dilate instead of constricting. What does this finding represent?
options: A positive relative afferent pupillary defect
originalOptions: A positive relative afferent pupillary defect
questions.correctAnswer: C) A positive relative afferent pupillary defect
questions.correctAnswerText: A positive relative afferent pupillary defect
questions.explanation: A positive RAPD occurs when light swung to the affected eye produces paradoxical dilation of both pupils because that eye has a reduced afferent signal reaching the brain. This paradoxical response distinguishes RAPD from a normal reflex, where both pupils would constrict equally.

questions.text: A glaucoma patient with a known sulfa allergy needs additional intraocular pressure control. Which medication should be avoided?
questions.explanation: Acetazolamide is a sulfonamide-derived carbonic anhydrase inhibitor, so it must be avoided in patients with a documented sulfa allergy. The other listed glaucoma agents belong to different drug classes and do not carry this cross-reactivity risk.

questions.text: A patient has limited abduction of one eye on examination, with normal movement in all other directions. Which cranial nerve is most likely affected?
questions.explanation: Isolated limitation of abduction in one eye reflects lateral rectus weakness, which is supplied by the abducent (CN VI) nerve. The oculomotor and trochlear nerves control other extraocular muscles and would not cause this isolated abduction deficit.

questions.text: A patient on long-term antimalarial therapy develops progressive visual field loss. Which drug is most associated with this retinal complication?
questions.explanation: Chloroquine and related antimalarials are well recognized to cause retinal toxicity with long-term use, which can lead to progressive visual field loss. Roaccutane instead causes severe ocular dryness, and topical steroids are more associated with cataract and raised intraocular pressure.

questions.text: On confrontation visual field testing, the examiner notes the patient's blind spot is larger than expected. What should this finding raise suspicion for?
options: Optic nerve disorder such as papilledema
originalOptions: Optic nerve disorder such as papilledema
questions.correctAnswer: A) Optic nerve disorder such as papilledema
questions.correctAnswerText: Optic nerve disorder such as papilledema
questions.explanation: The physiological blind spot should have a fixed, unenlarged size on confrontation testing; enlargement points toward an optic nerve disorder such as papilledema or optic neuritis. A refractive error or cataract would not selectively enlarge the blind spot.

questions.text: A patient presents with bilateral limitation of abduction on eye movement examination. Which location is most likely responsible?
questions.explanation: Bilateral limitation of abduction suggests a lesion affecting both abducent nerve pathways simultaneously, which localizes to the brainstem rather than a single peripheral nerve. Chiasmal or occipital lesions instead produce visual field defects rather than isolated bilateral eye movement restriction.

questions.text: A patient with a history of chronic topical steroid use for ocular inflammation develops gradually worsening vision. Which type of cataract is most classically associated with steroid use?
questions.explanation: Chronic steroid use is classically linked to posterior subcapsular cataract formation, which characteristically causes early visual disturbance despite a relatively clear lens periphery. Nuclear sclerotic and cortical cataracts are more typically age-related rather than steroid-induced.

questions.text: During ophthalmoscopy, the examiner needs to determine whether the fundus image belongs to the right or left eye. Which anatomical relationship allows this distinction?
options: Position of the optic disc relative to the fovea
Position of the optic disc relative to the macula
originalOptions: Position of the optic disc relative to the fovea
Position of the optic disc relative to the macula
questions.correctAnswer: E) Position of the optic disc relative to the macula
questions.correctAnswerText: Position of the optic disc relative to the macula
questions.explanation: The optic disc lies nasal to the macula, so identifying which side the disc appears on relative to the macula tells the examiner whether the image is of the right or left eye. Vessel size and choroidal color do not vary in a way that reliably indicates laterality.

questions.text: A patient presents with progressive loss of the outer visual field in both eyes, sparing the central visual field. Which underlying process is most likely responsible?
questions.correctAnswer: C) Compression at the optic chiasm
questions.explanation: Bitemporal visual field loss reflects damage to the crossing nasal retinal fibers at the optic chiasm, most commonly from a compressive lesion such as a pituitary adenoma. Retinal detachment and AMD instead produce peripheral or central field loss localized to one eye rather than a bitemporal pattern.

questions.text: Psychology Which of the following best defines psychiatry as a medical specialty?
options: A subspecialty of neurology focused on brain imaging
The branch of medicine concerned with prevention, diagnosis, treatment, and rehabilitation of mental disorders
A field limited to prescribing medication for behavioral complaints
A discipline concerned only with social counseling services
A field limited to managing psychotic disorders in hospitals
originalOptions: A subspecialty of neurology focused on brain imaging
The branch of medicine concerned with prevention, diagnosis, treatment, and rehabilitation of mental disorders
A field limited to prescribing medication for behavioral complaints
A discipline concerned only with social counseling services
A field limited to managing psychotic disorders in hospitals
questions.correctAnswer: B) The branch of medicine concerned with prevention, diagnosis, treatment, and rehabilitation of mental disorders
questions.correctAnswerText: The branch of medicine concerned with prevention, diagnosis, treatment, and rehabilitation of mental disorders
questions.explanation: Psychiatry is defined by its four core functions: prevention, diagnosis, treatment, and rehabilitation. Because psychiatrists are physicians, they integrate biological, psychological, and social factors rather than being limited to medication or counseling alone.

questions.text: Why is psychiatry considered clinically important according to the lecture?
options: It only becomes relevant during inpatient hospital stays
It is exclusively concerned with rare genetic brain disorders
Mental illness increases mortality from physical disease
It has no measurable effect on outcomes in physical illness
It is relevant mainly to forensic and legal settings
originalOptions: It only becomes relevant during inpatient hospital stays
It is exclusively concerned with rare genetic brain disorders
Mental illness increases mortality from physical disease
It has no measurable effect on outcomes in physical illness
It is relevant mainly to forensic and legal settings
questions.correctAnswer: C) Mental illness increases mortality from physical disease
questions.correctAnswerText: Mental illness increases mortality from physical disease
questions.explanation: The lecture emphasizes that roughly 1 in 8 people worldwide are affected by mental disorders, depression is the second leading cause of disability after cardiovascular disease, and mental illness worsens outcomes in physical disease, underscoring why every physician practices some psychiatry.

questions.text: According to the biopsychosocial model, mental illness is best understood as:
options: A condition with a single dominant biological cause
A purely psychological phenomenon unrelated to biology
A result of interaction between biological, psychological, and social domains
A social construct with no biological basis
A condition determined solely by genetics
originalOptions: A condition with a single dominant biological cause
A purely psychological phenomenon unrelated to biology
A result of interaction between biological, psychological, and social domains
A social construct with no biological basis
A condition determined solely by genetics
questions.correctAnswer: C) A result of interaction between biological, psychological, and social domains
questions.correctAnswerText: A result of interaction between biological, psychological, and social domains
questions.explanation: The biopsychosocial model frames mental illness as multifactorial, arising from the interaction of biological factors (genetics, brain function, neurotransmitters), psychological factors (personality, coping, trauma), and social factors (family, culture, employment).

questions.text: Which statement correctly distinguishes mental health from mental illness?
options: Mental health is the complete absence of any disorder
Mental health includes emotional well-being and social functioning
Mental illness and mental health cannot coexist
Mental health refers only to absence of psychotic symptoms
Mental health is determined by occupational performance alone
originalOptions: Mental health is the complete absence of any disorder
Mental health includes emotional well-being and social functioning
Mental illness and mental health cannot coexist
Mental health refers only to absence of psychotic symptoms
Mental health is determined by occupational performance alone
questions.correctAnswer: B) Mental health includes emotional well-being and social functioning
questions.correctAnswerText: Mental health includes emotional well-being and social functioning
questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly states mental health is not merely the absence of disease; it is a positive state encompassing emotional well-being, psychological resilience, social functioning, occupational ability, and stress coping.

questions.text: For a condition to be classified as a mental disorder, which combination of features must generally be present?
options: Disturbance in cognition or behavior, with no distress required
Any unusual thought, regardless of its effect on functioning
Disturbance in a core domain plus significant distress or impairment
A diagnosis made only after psychiatric hospitalization
originalOptions: Disturbance in cognition or behavior, with no distress required
Any unusual thought, regardless of its effect on functioning
Disturbance in a core domain plus significant distress or impairment
A diagnosis made only after psychiatric hospitalization
questions.correctAnswer: C) Disturbance in a core domain plus significant distress or impairment
questions.correctAnswerText: Disturbance in a core domain plus significant distress or impairment
questions.explanation: A mental disorder requires both a disturbance in one of the core domains (cognition, emotion, behavior, interpersonal functioning) AND that this disturbance produces significant distress or impairment affecting social life, relationships, work, or studying.

questions.text: A patient describes an anxiety symptom in which the world suddenly feels vivid, "like a rainbow," yet the sensation is distressing and overwhelming rather than pleasant. This is most consistent with which symptom?
options: Fear, with the world perceived as uniformly dark
Panic, which can present as a heightened, vivid perception of reality
Elation, as a pure mood symptom without anxiety
originalOptions: Fear, with the world perceived as uniformly dark
Panic, which can present as a heightened, vivid perception of reality
Elation, as a pure mood symptom without anxiety
questions.correctAnswer: B) Panic, which can present as a heightened, vivid perception of reality
questions.correctAnswerText: Panic, which can present as a heightened, vivid perception of reality
questions.explanation: The lecture notes panic can sometimes present as a vivid, almost euphoric perception of the world (colorful, "life feels great") rather than only the classic dark, threatening perception seen in fear, making panic recognizable in atypical presentations.

questions.text: Hallucinations and delusions are classified under which domains of the Mental Status Examination, respectively?
questions.explanation: Hallucinations (perceiving things that are not present) fall under perception, while delusions (fixed false beliefs) fall under thought content. The lecture separates these domains within the MSE, alongside thought process, cognition, insight, and judgment.

questions.text: Which of the following is emphasized as essential to always assess as part of psychiatric risk assessment?
options: Only suicide risk, addressed separately from other risks
Only substance use, as the most common comorbidity
Suicide, self-harm, homicidal thoughts, psychosis, and abuse
Only homicidal ideation in forensic patients
Only domestic violence in female patients
originalOptions: Only suicide risk, addressed separately from other risks
Only substance use, as the most common comorbidity
Suicide, self-harm, homicidal thoughts, psychosis, and abuse
Only homicidal ideation in forensic patients
Only domestic violence in female patients
questions.correctAnswer: C) Suicide, self-harm, homicidal thoughts, psychosis, and abuse
questions.correctAnswerText: Suicide, self-harm, homicidal thoughts, psychosis, and abuse
questions.explanation: Risk assessment is part of every psychiatric evaluation and must always cover this full set of areas, since each carries potentially serious and preventable consequences if missed during assessment.

questions.text: Which pairing correctly identifies the brain stimulation therapies discussed among treatment modalities?
questions.explanation: The lecture lists brain stimulation therapies as a distinct treatment modality alongside psychotherapy and medication, specifically naming ECT (Electroconvulsive Therapy) and TMS (Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation).

questions.text: According to the lecture, stigma in psychiatry manifests in three primary forms. Which set correctly represents these?
options: Structural, familial, and public stigma only
originalOptions: Structural, familial, and public stigma only
questions.correctAnswer: A) Public, self, and structural stigma
questions.explanation: Stigma manifests as public stigma (societal discrimination and stereotypes), self-stigma (internalizing negative views leading to shame), and structural stigma (institutional policies limiting rights or care), together contributing to delayed treatment and poor adherence.

questions.text: Radiology On CT, an acute intracranial hemorrhage (0–3 days old) typically appears as which of the following?
questions.explanation: Fresh blood is denser than brain parenchyma, so acute hemorrhage (0–3 days) appears hyperdense on CT. This density evolves predictably over time, becoming isodense in the subacute phase and hypodense once chronic.

questions.text: A hemorrhage older than 4 weeks (chronic stage) typically appears on CT as which of the following?
questions.explanation: As clot breaks down and fluid content increases, hemorrhage density decreases over time: hyperdense when acute, isodense when subacute, and hypodense once chronic (over 4 weeks). Confusing the acute and chronic stages is a common exam trap since the pattern is opposite to what students expect.

questions.text: Which early CT finding is characteristic of hyperacute ischemic stroke (6–24 hours)?
questions.explanation: In the early hyperacute phase of ischemia, cytotoxic edema causes cortical hypodensity with loss of grey-white matter differentiation and gyral effacement. This is an early, subtle sign, while volume loss and calcification only appear in the chronic stage.

questions.text: Vasogenic edema on brain imaging is classically seen surrounding which of the following?
questions.explanation: Vasogenic edema classically surrounds tumors, abscesses, and hemorrhages due to disruption of the blood-brain barrier. This is distinct from cytotoxic edema, which occurs in ischemia and affects both grey and white matter directly.

questions.text: Which radiological feature helps distinguish pulmonary consolidation from lobar collapse?
questions.explanation: Consolidation shows airspace opacification with air bronchograms and no volume loss or fissure displacement, since the airways remain patent. Collapse, in contrast, shows volume loss and fissure displacement toward the affected lobe, with no air bronchograms.

questions.text: Which imaging modality is preferred for diagnosing acute cholecystitis?
questions.explanation: Ultrasound is the preferred modality for cholecystitis, showing a distended gallbladder, thickened wall, calculi, and pericholecystic fluid. It is also the preferred first study for bowel obstruction and appendicitis per the lecture.

questions.text: In a patient with suspected bowel perforation, which radiograph is most sensitive for detecting free intraperitoneal air?
questions.explanation: An erect chest x-ray is the most sensitive plain radiograph for detecting free intraperitoneal gas, appearing as lucent air beneath the diaphragm. Abdominal signs such as the Rigler (double wall) sign and football sign are also associated findings of pneumoperitoneum.

questions.text: A barium enema shows an "apple core" sign with eccentric wall thickening in the descending colon. This finding is characteristic of which condition?
questions.explanation: The apple core sign reflects eccentric, annular wall thickening typical of colorectal carcinoma and is often associated with luminal obstruction. This classic contrast-study sign was specifically highlighted in the lecture for colorectal cancer.

questions.text: What is the earliest radiological finding in ankylosing spondylitis?
questions.explanation: Sacroiliitis, seen as widening or narrowing of the sacroiliac joints, is the earliest radiological finding in ankylosing spondylitis. Bamboo spine, dagger spine, and vertebral squaring are later findings that develop as the disease progresses.

questions.text: A 45-year-old woman's screening mammogram is classified as BI-RADS 4. What is the appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: BI-RADS 4 indicates a suspicious finding with roughly 2–94% probability of malignancy, so biopsy should be considered. This contrasts with BI-RADS 3 (probably benign, short-interval follow-up) and BI-RADS 1–2 (normal or benign, routine follow-up).

allQuestions.text: A young male presents with unilateral epistaxis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
allQuestions.explanation: Unilateral epistaxis in a young male (adolescent) is a classic presentation of juvenile nasal angiofibroma (JNA), a vascular tumor requiring rule-out by nasal endoscopy. Septal deviation and foreign body typically cause obstruction rather than recurrent bleeding, and allergic rhinitis/nasal polyps are usually bilateral.

allQuestions.text: A patient has bone conduction better than air conduction on the right ear (negative Rinne). On Weber's test, sound is heard better in the right ear. What is the diagnosis?
allQuestions.explanation: A negative Rinne test (bone conduction > air conduction) on the right ear indicates a conductive problem in that ear, and Weber lateralizing to the same (right) ear confirms this, since sound lateralizes toward the affected ear in conductive hearing loss. In sensorineural loss, Rinne would remain positive (air > bone) and Weber would lateralize to the opposite, better-hearing ear.

allQuestions.text: What tuning fork frequency is used for hearing tests?
allQuestions.explanation: The standard tuning fork used for Weber's and Rinne's tests is 512 Hz, providing a balance between adequate duration of vibration and clinical accuracy. Lower frequencies (128–256 Hz) are felt more than heard, while higher frequencies decay too quickly for reliable bedside testing.

allQuestions.text: A child presents with a midline neck lump that moves when they stick out their tongue. What is the diagnosis?
allQuestions.explanation: A thyroglossal cyst is a midline neck lump that characteristically moves upward on tongue protrusion due to its embryological connection to the foramen cecum. Thyroid isthmus swelling moves with swallowing but not tongue protrusion, dermoid cysts are non-tender and mobile without this movement, and branchial cysts are lateral, not midline.

allQuestions.text: A 55-year-old male smoker presents with hoarseness of voice persisting for 3 weeks. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
originalOptions: Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
allQuestions.correctAnswer: D) Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
allQuestions.correctAnswerText: Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
allQuestions.explanation: Any smoker with hoarseness lasting more than 3 weeks is a red flag requiring urgent ENT referral for fiberoptic endoscopy to look for laryngeal tumors, as emphasized directly in the lecture. Antibiotics, PPIs, or reassurance risk delaying diagnosis of a potentially malignant cause.

allQuestions.text: A young male teacher presents with hoarseness of voice for 3 weeks with a history of prolonged vocal strain. Laryngoscopy reveals a smooth, unilateral lesion on the vocal cord. What is the most likely diagnosis?
allQuestions.explanation: A vocal cord polyp typically presents as a unilateral, smooth lesion following a single episode of vocal strain or trauma, unlike nodules which are bilateral and related to chronic voice abuse (e.g., singers/teachers using their voice repetitively).

allQuestions.text: Which of the following is NOT an early sign of death?
allQuestions.explanation: Livor mortis, rigor mortis, and pallor mortis are recognized postmortem changes that develop progressively over time and are used forensically to estimate time and circumstances of death. Loss of peripheral pulse occurs immediately at the moment of cardiac arrest rather than developing afterward, so it is a clinical sign of death, not a postmortem change. This distinction is why the lecture emphasized rigor mortis, hypostasis, and decomposition as the key diagnostic postmortem changes.

allQuestions.text: Traumatic asphyxia results from severe injury to which part of the body?
allQuestions.explanation: Traumatic asphyxia is defined as compression on the chest and abdomen by external force, impairing the mechanical ability to breathe. This differs from strangulation/hanging (neck level) and smothering (mouth and nose level), which are other forms of violent asphyxia acting at different anatomical levels.

allQuestions.text: In forensic medicine, strangulation can be categorized into which manner of death?
allQuestions.explanation: Strangulation (compression of neck structures by ligature or hands) is mostly homicidal, unlike hanging, which is mostly suicidal but can be accidental or homicidal. This distinction in manner of death is forensically important because it directly affects legal classification and investigation of the case.

allQuestions.text: Which type of injury is not caused by a blunt object but rather by a sharp object, typically resulting in clean, well-defined wound edges?
allQuestions.explanation: Incisions/cut wounds are caused by a clean, sharp-edged object such as a knife or glass splinter, producing regular edges, clear margins, and no tissue bridging. In contrast, lacerations, abrasions, and contusions all result from blunt trauma and typically show irregular edges, contused margins, and tissue bridging.

allQuestions.text: What is the approximate time window during which myocardial cells can be harvested after cardiac arrest?
allQuestions.explanation: Different tissues and organs remain viable for different durations after death, since cells do not die instantly and organs vary in their tolerance to anoxia (the lecture notes the brain survives only 5-7 minutes and the kidney about 45 minutes). This variability explains why timing is critical for organ/tissue donation and harvesting after cardiac arrest.

allQuestions.text: Stab wounds can be classified into which of the following categories?
allQuestions.explanation: Stab wounds can result from a purely sharp instrument, a blunt instrument, or a blunt object with a pointed tip (as illustrated by the example of a worker falling onto a round-tipped iron rod), since what matters legally and clinically is the depth and mechanism of penetration rather than the object's overall sharpness. This is why all three categories are considered valid classifications of stab wounds in forensic assessment.

allQuestions.text: Which of the following is NOT a cause of an incisional (cut) wound?
allQuestions.explanation: Incisions/cut wounds are caused by a clean, sharp-edged object such as a knife, razor, or glass splinter/broken glass. A rock is a blunt object, so injury from it produces a laceration (irregular tear-like wound), not an incision — making it the exception among the choices.

allQuestions.text: The mechanism of rigor mortis involves decreased ATP leading to which direct biochemical event in muscle cells?
options: Binding of actin and myosin fibres into a gel
Calcium efflux from the sarcoplasmic reticulum
originalOptions: Binding of actin and myosin fibres into a gel
Calcium efflux from the sarcoplasmic reticulum
allQuestions.correctAnswer: B) Binding of actin and myosin fibres into a gel
allQuestions.correctAnswerText: Binding of actin and myosin fibres into a gel
allQuestions.explanation: After death, anaerobic respiration causes lactic acid buildup and increasing acidity; combined with low ATP, this causes the actin and myosin fibres to bind together and form a stiff gel, producing rigor mortis. This is why rigor mortis preserves and reflects the position the person died in, rather than reflecting later body movement.

allQuestions.text: Smothering is best defined as which of the following?
options: Compression on neck structures by ligature
Compression on neck structures by the weight of the body
Closure of the nostrils and mouth at the same time from outside
Compression on the chest and abdomen by external force
Occlusion of the respiratory passages from inside
originalOptions: Compression on neck structures by ligature
Compression on neck structures by the weight of the body
Closure of the nostrils and mouth at the same time from outside
Compression on the chest and abdomen by external force
Occlusion of the respiratory passages from inside
allQuestions.correctAnswer: C) Closure of the nostrils and mouth at the same time from outside
allQuestions.correctAnswerText: Closure of the nostrils and mouth at the same time from outside
allQuestions.explanation: Smothering occurs at the level of the mouth and nose, involving external closure of both openings simultaneously, and is mostly suicidal though it can be homicidal. This differs from gagging, which produces a similar effect but from inside the mouth (e.g., a cloth pushed into the mouth and pharynx).

allQuestions.text: A wound characterized by irregular, tear-like edges caused by blunt trauma is best classified as which type of injury?
allQuestions.explanation: Lacerations are irregular tear-like wounds caused by blunt trauma, requiring a firm structure (such as bone) beneath the skin to allow the tissue to tear and the skin to open. This contrasts with incisions, which have regular edges and clear margins from a sharp object, and abrasions, which are superficial scrapes of the epidermis only.

allQuestions.text: The color of skin discoloration seen in postmortem hypostasis is primarily determined by which factor?
options: The ambient temperature at the death scene
The position in which the body was found
The chemical state of hemoglobin molecules
The total blood volume remaining in the vessels
The time elapsed since cardiac arrest
originalOptions: The ambient temperature at the death scene
The position in which the body was found
The chemical state of hemoglobin molecules
The total blood volume remaining in the vessels
The time elapsed since cardiac arrest
allQuestions.correctAnswer: C) The chemical state of hemoglobin molecules
allQuestions.correctAnswerText: The chemical state of hemoglobin molecules
allQuestions.explanation: Postmortem hypostasis color depends on the chemical state of hemoglobin (e.g., carboxyhemoglobin, methemoglobin), which is why, for example, carbon monoxide poisoning classically produces a pink or light red discoloration rather than the usual bluish-purple color. This makes hypostasis color a useful clue for suspecting certain causes of death, such as CO poisoning.

allQuestions.text: If a patient has a visual acuity of 6/12, what does this mean?
options: They can see at 6 meters what a person with normal vision can see at 12 meters
They can see at 12 meters what a person with normal vision can see at 6 meters
Their vision is better than normal, resolving details invisible to the average eye at 6 meters
They have normal visual acuity equivalent to 6/6
They can only perceive hand movements at a distance of 6 meters
originalOptions: They can see at 6 meters what a person with normal vision can see at 12 meters
They can see at 12 meters what a person with normal vision can see at 6 meters
Their vision is better than normal, resolving details invisible to the average eye at 6 meters
They have normal visual acuity equivalent to 6/6
They can only perceive hand movements at a distance of 6 meters
allQuestions.correctAnswer: A) They can see at 6 meters what a person with normal vision can see at 12 meters
allQuestions.correctAnswerText: They can see at 6 meters what a person with normal vision can see at 12 meters
allQuestions.explanation: 6/12 = test distance/normal distance, meaning reduced acuity, not enhanced. B reverses the fraction (would mean better-than-normal vision), C and D wrongly describe normal or supranormal vision, and E describes near-blindness, far worse than 6/12.

allQuestions.text: This test is used to asses (The image shows: A circle of green dots containing the number 12 made of orange dots) ?
allQuestions.explanation: This is an Ishihara pseudoisochromatic plate, used specifically for color vision testing. A uses the Snellen chart, B uses a light source, C uses confrontation/perimetry, and E uses contrast charts — none use colored dot plates.

allQuestions.text: What condition causes acute painful loss of vision?
allQuestions.explanation: Only angle-closure glaucoma is both acute and painful. A and E are acute but painless, while C and D are painless and gradual, not acute.

allQuestions.text: All are causes of this sign except (The image shows: A red eye) ?
allQuestions.explanation: Macular edema is a retinal/central vision problem, not a cause of ocular redness. A–D all directly cause a red eye through conjunctival, corneal, or intraocular inflammation/hemorrhage.

allQuestions.text: What is the term for pupils of different sizes?
allQuestions.explanation: Anisocoria specifically means unequal pupil size. Nystagmus is involuntary eye movement, ptosis is lid drooping, diplopia is double vision, and RAPD is an abnormal light reflex — none describe pupil size difference.

allQuestions.text: What is the visual field exam called?
allQuestions.explanation: The confrontation test is the bedside visual field exam. A tests color, B tests for RAPD, D tests acuity, and E examines the fundus — all test different functions.

allQuestions.text: What are the three components involved in accommodation?
allQuestions.correctAnswer: A) Accommodation, pupillary constriction, and convergence
allQuestions.explanation: The near reflex triad is lens accommodation + pupillary constriction + convergence. B and C swap in divergence (the opposite of convergence), D swaps in dilation (opposite of constriction), and E replaces accommodation with visual acuity, which isn't part of the triad.

allQuestions.text: The limbus houses germinative stem cells that maintain the corneal epithelium. What is the primary function of this layer?
options: Anchoring the epithelium via desmosomes only
Providing mechanical strength via collagen fibrils
Regeneration and proliferation of epithelial cells
originalOptions: Anchoring the epithelium via desmosomes only
Providing mechanical strength via collagen fibrils
Regeneration and proliferation of epithelial cells
allQuestions.correctAnswer: E) Regeneration and proliferation of epithelial cells
allQuestions.correctAnswerText: Regeneration and proliferation of epithelial cells
allQuestions.explanation: Limbal stem cells continuously regenerate the corneal epithelium, allowing rapid repair after abrasion by cell spreading and proliferation. A is incomplete, as anchoring also involves hemidesmosomes and fibrils, not desmosomes alone. B is wrong — the endothelium instead repairs by cell spreading alone with loss of density, no regeneration. C and D describe Bowman's layer and the trabecular meshwork, unrelated structures.

allQuestions.text: Which of the following statements best describes Alogia?
options: It refers to speech that lacks content but is delivered fluently and without hesitation
It involves both poverty in speech output and poverty in the content of speech
It refers to speech that is rich in content but delivered in an impoverished, halting manner
It refers to an excessive amount of speech with overly detailed content
It refers to speech that is normal in amount but disorganized in structure
originalOptions: It refers to speech that lacks content but is delivered fluently and without hesitation
It involves both poverty in speech output and poverty in the content of speech
It refers to speech that is rich in content but delivered in an impoverished, halting manner
It refers to an excessive amount of speech with overly detailed content
It refers to speech that is normal in amount but disorganized in structure
allQuestions.correctAnswer: B) It involves both poverty in speech output and poverty in the content of speech
allQuestions.correctAnswerText: It involves both poverty in speech output and poverty in the content of speech
allQuestions.explanation: Alogia (poverty of speech) is a negative symptom involving reduction in both the amount of speech produced and the richness of its content. This distinguishes it from disorders of speech delivery alone, where content may still be preserved.

allQuestions.text: Which of the following is NOT a component of thought content?
allQuestions.explanation: Thought content covers delusions, obsessions, overvalued ideas, and suicidal/homicidal ideation. Hallucinations are a disturbance of perception, not thought content, so they are assessed separately under the perception domain of the MSE.
